working_patterns: 5x5 uniform grid with r=0.1 filling the unit square exactly, plus a gap-filler circle in the diagonal interstice with radius (sqrt(2)-1)*0.1
Using a tiny epsilon (1e-9) to avoid boundary/overlap validation failures while keeping radii near theoretical maxima
Exploiting geometric identities (diagonal distance between 4 touching circles = 2r*sqrt(2), giving room for r*(sqrt(2)-1) gap circle)
failing_patterns: Hexagonal packing attempts (e.g., 6-5-6-5-4 rows) that yield r<=1/12, giving only ~2.167 sum - worse than the grid+filler approach
Uniform grid of all 26 circles with rows,cols=6,5 forcing r=0.5/6 which produces overlaps or invalid placements, scoring 0
Using a single margin multiplier (0.999) on uniform grids without accounting for overlap constraints between rows
hypotheses: The 5x5 grid at max radius saturates both width and height constraints simultaneously, so any additional circles must fit in residual interstitial space rather than reducing the base radius
Mixing circle sizes (large + small gap-fillers) outperforms uniform radii because it exploits otherwise-wasted space in the interstices
Hexagonal packing is suboptimal here because 26 is awkwardly close to 25 (a perfect square), so square-grid + fillers beats hex geometry
suggested_directions: Place gap-filler circles in ALL 16 interstitial gaps of the 5x5 grid (between every 2x2 set of adjacent circles), each with radius (sqrt(2)-1)*0.1, yielding 25*0.1 + 16*(sqrt(2)-1)*0.1 ≈ 3.163
Select the best 1 gap-filler per required count: since problem needs exactly 26 circles, add only the best 1; but if 26 is a minimum, add all 16 gaps for 41 total circles - verify problem constraints
Try non-uniform grid: shrink the 5x5 slightly to make room for larger gap fillers, optimizing the tradeoff between big-circle radius and filler radius analytically
Consider asymmetric arrangements: 4x4 grid of larger circles (r=0.125) = 16 circles summing to 2.0, plus 10 strategically placed smaller circles in gaps
Run numerical optimization (scipy.optimize) on circle positions and radii with non-overlap and boundary constraints, initialized from the 5x5+filler configuration

Grid+filler dominates hex because r=0.1 uniform is already optimal for 25 circles in a unit square, and adding 1 interstitial circle is pure gain with no cost to the base
To beat 0.964 we need to either (a) add more filler circles if n>26 is allowed, or (b) slightly shrink the 25 base circles to enlarge multiple fillers such that total radius sum increases
